items.prompt: I need to compare total sales across different regions to understand which markets are driving the most revenue. Please sort the regions from highest to lowest sales so the ranking is immediately clear. This should help inform where we focus our expansion efforts.

total_sales.group_effects: North America and Asia-Pacific highest, Europe mid, others lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has data for every month so total sales can be aggregated and ranked by region.

items.prompt: Can you show how revenue changes month over month throughout the year? I want to identify any seasonal trends or recurring patterns in monthly revenue. This will help us plan promotions and inventory more effectively.

revenue.group_effects: North and West slightly higher than South and East

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has one entry per month and embed clear seasonal spikes (e.g., November-December) and dips (e.g., February) to highlight recurring patterns.

items.prompt: I’d like to understand the relationship between ad spend and revenue across campaigns. Plotting these two metrics should help reveal whether higher spend is actually driving better returns. This will guide how we allocate budget across channels.

ad_spend.group_effects: Search and Social tend to have higher spend

revenue.group_effects: Search campaigns yield higher revenue efficiency, Display lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure a generally positive correlation between ad_spend and revenue, with some variability and a few outliers to reflect inefficient or high-performing campaigns.

items.prompt: Please show the distribution of customer ratings across all orders to assess overall satisfaction. I want to see how frequently different rating levels occur and whether most customers are satisfied. This will help identify if there are issues in specific rating ranges.

customer_rating.group_effects: Electronics and Home slightly higher ratings, Clothing slightly lower

df_spec.notes: Ratings should be discrete integers from 1 to 5 with a mild skew toward higher values to reflect generally satisfied customers.

items.prompt: Compare the average profit across product categories to see which segments are most profitable. This should help us identify where margins are strongest and where improvements are needed. Use the category dimension to break down the profit metric clearly.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Furniture higher on average

profit.group_effects: Electronics highest margins, Food & Beverage lowest

df_spec.notes: Ensure each category has a similar number of records so average profit comparisons are meaningful.

items.prompt: Show how sales have trended over time by month for each region so we can compare growth patterns. I want to see whether certain regions are accelerating faster than others. This will help prioritize regional sales strategies.

sales.group_effects: West and Central accelerate faster, South slower growth

df_spec.notes: Each region should have one record per month. Ensure differing growth slopes so some regions visibly accelerate faster over time.

items.prompt: Analyze delivery time across different shipping methods to understand consistency and delays. I’m particularly interested in the spread and any outliers in delivery time by shipping method. This will help evaluate which options provide the most reliable service.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: Overnight lowest, Express low, Two-Day moderate, Standard highest with more spread

df_spec.notes: Ensure each shipping method has sufficient samples and include occasional extreme delivery_time_days values to simulate delays and outliers.

items.prompt: Create a view of sales by month and region to identify seasonal peaks across different geographies. I want to quickly spot which regions perform best during specific months. This insight will help with regional planning and demand forecasting.

sales.group_effects: North America and Europe higher overall; Asia Pacific strong mid-year; Latin America and MEA lower baseline

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month-region combination exists; month column should align with date for easy pivoting into a heatmap.

items.prompt: Show the top 10 countries ranked by total revenue so we can identify our most important markets. Make it easy to compare how much each country contributes to overall revenue. This will support decisions on international growth priorities.

revenue.group_effects: United States and China highest, Europe mid-high, smaller markets lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each country has data for each month so total revenue can be aggregated and ranked to extract top 10 countries.

items.prompt: Plot conversion rate against traffic volume and break it down by customer segment to understand performance differences. I want to see whether higher traffic actually translates into better conversion across segments. This will help refine targeting and acquisition strategies.

traffic_volume.group_effects: Returning Customers and High-Value segments tend to have lower volume than New Visitors

conversions.group_effects: High-Value and Returning Customers have higher conversion efficiency

conversion_rate.group_effects: Higher for High-Value and Returning Customers, lower for Discount Seekers

df_spec.notes: Ensure conversion_rate is consistently computed from conversions and traffic_volume, with segment-based differences to highlight performance variation.

items.prompt: I want to track how daily average temperature changes over time to identify seasonal patterns and unusual spikes. Please plot temperature by date so we can see trends and fluctuations clearly. Highlight any periods where temperature deviates significantly from the norm.

df_spec.notes: Ensure clear seasonal pattern with occasional sharp deviations flagged via is_anomaly when deviation exceeds a threshold (e.g., ±5°C).

items.prompt: I need to understand how order values are distributed across all transactions. Plot the distribution of order value so we can see where most purchases fall and whether there are high-value outliers. This will help us better understand customer purchasing behavior.

order_value.group_effects: VIP customers tend to have higher order values

df_spec.notes: Order values should follow a right-skewed distribution (e.g., log-normal) with most values clustered at lower ranges and a small number of high-value outliers.

items.prompt: Compare profit margins across product categories and customer segments to identify where we are most profitable. Use a grouped view so differences between segments within each category are easy to interpret. I want to see which combinations drive the strongest margins.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Corporate segments tend to have higher revenue

cost.group_effects: Furniture has higher cost ratios, Clothing lower

profit_margin.group_effects: Clothing and Consumer segments tend to have higher margins, Furniture lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each product_category and customer_segment combination appears multiple times to allow grouped aggregation and clear margin comparison.

items.prompt: Analyze how monthly ad spend relates to revenue over time to evaluate marketing efficiency. Plot both metrics by month so we can compare trends and identify periods where spending did or did not translate into revenue growth. This should help assess ROI patterns.

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month has entries for all channels; revenue should loosely follow ad spend with occasional inefficiency periods where spend rises but revenue lags.

items.prompt: Examine the relationship between product price and units sold to understand demand sensitivity. Plot price against units sold across products to see if higher prices are reducing sales volume. This will help inform pricing strategy decisions.

price.group_effects: Electronics higher average price, Clothing lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure a clear inverse relationship between price and units_sold with some noise and a few outliers where high-priced items still achieve strong sales.

items.prompt: Assess how sales vary across different regions to identify consistency and outliers. Show the distribution of sales by region so we can compare variability and detect regions with unusually high or low performance. This will help prioritize regional strategies.

sales.group_effects: West and North have higher average sales, South has lower average, Central has higher variance with occasional extreme outliers

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has sufficient samples (at least 50) and include occasional extreme sales values in Central and West to test outlier detection in distributions.

items.prompt: I want to evaluate how revenue, profit, and ad spend are related to each other. Show the correlations between these metrics to identify strong positive or negative relationships. This will help us understand how marketing investment impacts financial outcomes.

ad_spend.group_effects: Search and Social higher spend than Email

revenue.group_effects: Search generates highest revenue efficiency

df_spec.notes: Ensure strong positive correlation between ad_spend and revenue, and moderate positive correlation between revenue and profit, with occasional negative profit at low efficiency points.

items.prompt: Track how monthly order volume changes over time for each customer type. Break out the trends by customer type so we can compare behavior patterns between segments. The goal is to identify which segments are growing or declining over time.

order_volume.group_effects: VIP highest and steadily increasing, Returning moderate growth, New slightly increasing, Churned declining over time

df_spec.notes: Ensure each customer_type has one record per month with distinct trend patterns to enable clear comparison of growth and decline.

items.prompt: Compare average delivery time across regions to identify where delays are most severe. Sort the regions so the slowest delivery times are easy to spot. This will help prioritize operational improvements in underperforming areas.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: West and South have higher averages, Central moderate, North and East lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure sufficient rows per region so average delivery time differences are visible when aggregated and sorted.

items.prompt: Analyze how revenue is distributed across different customer segments. Show the distribution of revenue with a clear distinction between segments so we can identify which groups contribute most and whether variability differs. This will inform targeting and retention strategies.

revenue.group_effects: Enterprise highest and most variable, Corporate mid-high, Small Business moderate, Consumer lowest

order_count.group_effects: Consumer and Small Business higher counts, Enterprise lower counts

df_spec.notes: Ensure each segment has sufficient spread and some high-end outliers, especially for Enterprise, to highlight variability in distribution plots.

items.prompt: Analyze how inventory levels relate to sales across different products to evaluate stock efficiency. I want to see whether higher inventory is actually driving higher sales or leading to overstock in certain product categories.

inventory_level.group_effects: Electronics and Home tend to have higher inventory levels

sales_units.group_effects: Clothing has higher sales relative to inventory, Electronics more variable

df_spec.notes: Ensure variation across categories so some show strong positive correlation while others exhibit overstock (high inventory but low sales). Include a few outliers with very high inventory and low sales.

items.prompt: Track daily website traffic over time and break it down by traffic source to understand channel performance. The goal is to identify which channels are driving the most consistent traffic and where fluctuations occur.

sessions.group_effects: Organic Search highest and most stable, Paid Search high but volatile, Social moderate with spikes, Direct steady mid-range

conversions.group_effects: Paid Search and Organic Search have higher conversion rates than Social and Direct

df_spec.notes: Ensure each date has entries for all traffic sources; include occasional spikes for campaigns and dips for weekends to reflect realistic traffic fluctuations.

items.prompt: Compare total sales across regions and product categories to understand contribution patterns. I want to see how each category performs within each region and which combinations drive the most revenue.

total_sales.group_effects: Electronics highest overall; North and West regions slightly higher revenue

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region-category combination appears multiple times so aggregations like sum or mean produce meaningful comparisons.

items.prompt: Examine the distribution of delivery times across different shipping types to assess service reliability. The goal is to identify which shipping methods have the most variability and longest delays.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: Overnight lowest mean with low variance; Two-Day low mean moderate variance; Expedited moderate mean moderate variance; Standard highest mean and highest variance
delivery_time_days.noise: high with occasional extreme outliers for Standard and Expedited

df_spec.notes: Ensure each shipping type has sufficient samples and visible spread; include occasional long delays especially for Standard to highlight variability.

items.prompt: Analyze order volume by weekday and hour to identify peak activity periods. This should help determine when customer demand is highest and inform staffing or promotion timing decisions.

order_count.group_effects: higher values during evening hours (17-21) and weekends; lower in early morning hours

df_spec.notes: Ensure each weekday has entries for all 24 hours; duplicate weeks can be simulated to reach row count while preserving patterns.

items.prompt: Track monthly revenue over time to understand overall growth trends. I want to clearly see how revenue changes from month to month and identify any periods of acceleration or decline.

df_spec.notes: Ensure revenue has realistic month-to-month continuity and that mom_change_pct reflects percent change from previous month.

items.prompt: Identify the top-performing products based on total sales to understand which items drive the most revenue. This will help prioritize inventory and marketing efforts toward high-performing products.

units_sold.group_effects: Electronics and Home products tend to have higher units sold

unit_price.group_effects: Electronics have higher prices, Apparel lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure multiple records per product to allow aggregation of total_sales for ranking top-performing products.

items.prompt: Explore the relationship between profit and revenue across products while accounting for order volume. The goal is to identify which products generate strong profits relative to their revenue and scale.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Furniture tend to have higher revenue

profit.group_effects: Clothing has lower margins, Electronics higher margins

order_volume.group_effects: Office Supplies have higher volume but lower revenue per order

df_spec.notes: Ensure variability in profit margins so some products have high revenue but low profit and vice versa; include a few negative-profit outliers.

items.prompt: Evaluate the distribution of monthly revenue growth rates to understand business volatility over time. I want to see how often we experience strong growth versus declines across different months.

revenue.group_effects: North and West slightly higher than South and East

df_spec.notes: Ensure growth rate is computed per region as month-over-month percent change, with occasional negative dips and positive spikes to reflect volatility.

items.prompt: Compare sales trends over time across different product categories to identify patterns and seasonality. I need to see each category separately to understand which ones are growing, declining, or behaving differently over time.

sales.trend: category-specific trends (Electronics upward, Clothing seasonal, Home stable, Sports slightly upward, Toys highly seasonal with spikes in Q4)
sales.group_effects: Electronics and Home higher baseline, Toys lower baseline except seasonal peaks

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month has entries for all product categories; include strong seasonal spikes for Toys and Clothing and gradual upward trend for Electronics.

items.prompt: I want to understand how our customer base is distributed across different segments. Please show the total customer count by segment so we can identify which groups are driving the most engagement. This will help prioritize targeting and retention strategies.

engagement_score.group_effects: Enterprise and Mid-Market slightly higher on average

df_spec.notes: Each row represents a unique customer; segment distribution should be uneven to make differences visible in counts.

items.prompt: We need to evaluate whether weather conditions are influencing sales performance. Plot sales against temperature across different dates to identify any correlation between the two variables. This should help us understand if warmer or colder days impact revenue trends.

sales_revenue.group_effects: South slightly higher, North slightly lower

df_spec.notes: Temperature should follow a seasonal sinusoidal pattern; sales should moderately increase with warmer temperatures but include noise and occasional outliers.

items.prompt: I’d like to analyze how profit varies across different regions. Show the distribution of profit by region so we can identify variability and any extreme outliers. This will help us spot underperforming or high-risk markets.

revenue.group_effects: North and West higher average revenue, Central lower

cost.group_effects: South and East have higher cost ratios

profit.group_effects: North and West more profitable, South more losses

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has a wide spread of profit values including negative values and a few extreme outliers to highlight variability.

items.prompt: We need a clear view of how conversion rate differs across device types and regions. Visualize conversion rate by device type and region to highlight any patterns or performance gaps. This will help optimize marketing and user experience strategies.

sessions.group_effects: Mobile highest volume across all regions, Tablet lowest

conversions.group_effects: Desktop slightly higher conversion efficiency than Mobile, Tablet varies by region

conversion_rate.group_effects: Europe and North America slightly higher rates; Mobile slightly lower than Desktop

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region-device combination appears multiple times so aggregation (mean conversion_rate) produces a meaningful heatmap.

items.prompt: Track how daily order volume has changed over time to identify trends or seasonality. Plot the number of orders by date with a smooth trend to make patterns easier to interpret. This will help with demand forecasting and operational planning.

df_spec.notes: Ensure daily continuity with clear weekly seasonality (higher on weekdays vs weekends) and compute rolling_7d_avg_orders from orders.

items.prompt: I want to break down total revenue by customer segment to understand contribution differences. Show how each segment contributes to overall revenue so we can compare their relative importance. This will support decisions around segment-focused strategies.

revenue.group_effects: Enterprise highest average revenue, Consumer lowest, Corporate and Small Business in between

df_spec.notes: Each row represents a transaction; aggregating revenue by customer_segment should produce clear differences in total contribution.

items.prompt: We need to understand the age distribution of our customer base. Visualize customer ages to see how they are spread across different age groups. This will help inform demographic targeting and product positioning.

age.group_effects: slight variation in average age by region

df_spec.notes: Age distribution should resemble a realistic customer base with more density between ages 25-55 and fewer observations at extremes.

items.prompt: Assess how efficiently our marketing spend is converting into results. Plot ad spend against conversion rate across campaigns or channels to identify any relationship. Include a trend to help determine whether higher spend is leading to better performance.

ad_spend.group_effects: higher spend on Google Ads and Facebook

df_spec.notes: Ensure a weak-to-moderate positive relationship between ad_spend and conversion_rate, with some diminishing returns and a few outliers where high spend yields low conversion efficiency.

items.prompt: I need to compare how profit trends evolve over time across different regions. Show monthly profit by region so we can evaluate regional performance patterns side by side. This will help identify which regions are improving or declining over time.

profit.group_effects: West and North generally higher, South more volatile, East gradual growth, Central stable

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has one record per month; include variation so some regions improve while others decline over time.

items.prompt: Identify the regions generating the highest order volumes. Show the top 5 regions ranked by total orders so we can quickly see where demand is strongest. This will help guide resource allocation and regional strategy decisions.

orders.group_effects: North, West, and Central have higher average orders; Southeast and Southwest lower

df_spec.notes: Each region should appear consistently across the date range so total orders can be aggregated and ranked to identify the top 5 regions.

items.prompt: I want to understand how shipping cost varies across different carriers. Plot the distribution of shipping costs segmented by carrier to identify which providers tend to be more expensive or inconsistent. This will help us evaluate cost efficiency and negotiate better rates.

shipping_cost.group_effects: DHL and FedEx slightly higher average costs, USPS lower but more variability, UPS moderate and consistent

df_spec.notes: Ensure each carrier has a wide spread of costs with distinct variance patterns so distribution plots clearly differentiate consistency and pricing levels.

items.prompt: Analyze the relationship between revenue and profit across different product categories. Create a scatter plot to see whether higher revenue consistently translates into higher profit by category. This will help identify underperforming categories with poor margins.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Home Goods higher on average, Toys lower

profit.group_effects: Clothing and Sports have higher margins, Electronics lower margins

df_spec.notes: Ensure variation in profit margins across categories so some high-revenue points show relatively low profit to highlight underperforming categories.

items.prompt: Track how inventory levels change over time to spot potential stockouts or overstock situations. Use a time series view of inventory by date to highlight trends and seasonality. This will support better supply chain and replenishment planning.

inventory_level.group_effects: Electronics slightly higher average inventory than Apparel

reorder_point.group_effects: Electronics higher reorder point than Apparel

df_spec.notes: Ensure each date has entries for both product categories; inventory should occasionally drop near or below reorder_point to simulate stockout risk and replenishment cycles.

items.prompt: Provide a clear view of how sales performance varies across regions and product categories. Use a heatmap to highlight high and low sales combinations by region and category. The goal is to quickly identify strong markets and underperforming segments.

sales.group_effects: Electronics and North/West regions tend to have higher sales; Furniture and South slightly lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region-category combination appears for every month so aggregation can produce a complete heatmap matrix.

items.prompt: Compare the distribution of order values across different customer types. Use this to understand whether certain segments, like new vs returning customers, tend to generate higher-value orders. This insight will help guide targeting and retention strategies.

order_value.group_effects: Loyal highest average, Returning mid, New lowest with wider spread
order_value.correlated_with: is_discounted (discounted orders slightly lower on average)

df_spec.notes: Ensure each customer_type has sufficient representation and a slightly different distribution shape to make comparisons meaningful.

items.prompt: Examine how revenue trends evolve month over month across different years. Plot monthly revenue with clear markers and distinguish each year to compare seasonal patterns. This will help identify growth trends and recurring peaks or dips.

revenue.group_effects: later years have higher baseline revenue
revenue.correlated_with: month (seasonal peaks in mid and end of year)

df_spec.notes: Ensure each year has all 12 months. Include clear seasonal patterns such as peaks around mid-year and year-end to enable comparison across years.

items.prompt: Compare total profit across countries to identify which markets are underperforming. Display profit by country in ascending order so it's easy to spot the weakest contributors. This will help prioritize regions that need attention or cost optimization.

revenue.group_effects: USA, Germany, and UK slightly higher; India and Brazil slightly lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure multiple rows per country so total profit aggregation is meaningful and allows sorting countries by total profit.

items.prompt: Evaluate delivery performance by analyzing delivery time across different regions. Plot the distribution of delivery times segmented by region to identify delays or inconsistencies. This will help pinpoint areas where logistics improvements are needed.

delivery_time_hours.group_effects: South and West have higher averages and more variability; Central is fastest; occasional extreme delays in all regions

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has a wide spread of delivery times with some long-tail delays to highlight distribution differences.

items.prompt: Explore how website traffic relates to conversions and revenue generation. Create a scatter plot of traffic versus conversions, using revenue to indicate the size of each point. This will help identify high-value segments and optimize marketing performance.

traffic.group_effects: Paid Search and Organic Search tend to have higher traffic

conversions.group_effects: Email and Referral have higher conversion efficiency

revenue.group_effects: Paid Search and Email yield higher revenue per conversion

df_spec.notes: Ensure a clear positive relationship between traffic and conversions, with revenue scaling nonlinearly to create visible variation in point sizes.

items.prompt: Break down sales performance by product category across different regions. Use faceted bar charts to compare how each category performs within each region side by side. The goal is to identify regional preferences and category-level growth opportunities.

units_sold.group_effects: Electronics and Clothing higher volume; North and West slightly higher

avg_price.group_effects: Electronics highest, Beauty and Clothing lower

sales.group_effects: Regions have distinct category preferences (e.g., Sports stronger in West, Beauty in South)

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has all product categories for every month to enable faceted bar comparisons and consistent grouping.

items.prompt: Analyze monthly revenue and profit over the past year to understand how growth is impacting margins. Plot both metrics by month using dual y-axes so we can compare their trends clearly. Highlight any periods where revenue increased but profit lagged to identify margin pressure.

revenue.group_effects: Enterprise and International higher, SMB lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure some months have rising revenue with flat or declining profit to trigger margin_pressure_flag for highlighting.

items.prompt: Build a dashboard view comparing total sales across regions alongside the overall sales trend over time. Use bars to show regional sales and a line to show total sales by month in a separate subplot. This should help identify which regions are driving overall performance changes.

regional_sales.group_effects: North and West higher, Central moderate, South and East slightly lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month includes all regions so regional aggregation aligns with total_sales for line chart.

items.prompt: Track revenue by month to identify any significant declines that may need investigation. Plot the time series and annotate points where revenue drops sharply compared to the previous period. The goal is to quickly pinpoint when and how often these declines occur.

df_spec.notes: Ensure several months have significant negative revenue_change_pct (e.g., below -0.15) to trigger sharp_drop=true for annotation.

items.prompt: Compare total sales across product categories and customer segments to understand contribution mix. Create a stacked bar chart showing sales by category with segments layered within each bar. Ensure the legend clearly distinguishes segments so we can assess which groups drive category performance.

sales.group_effects: Electronics highest overall; Corporate segment slightly higher spend per transaction

df_spec.notes: Ensure each product_category and customer_segment combination appears multiple times so aggregation can produce meaningful stacked bars.

items.prompt: Evaluate the relationship between ad spend and revenue across marketing channels or campaigns. Plot a scatter chart with ad spend on one axis and revenue on the other, and include a regression line to highlight the trend. This will help determine how effectively spend is translating into revenue.

ad_spend.group_effects: Search and Social tend to have higher spend than Email

revenue.group_effects: Search has strongest return, Display weakest

df_spec.notes: Ensure a generally positive correlation between ad_spend and revenue with some variability and a few outliers to make regression meaningful. Distribute data across all channel and campaign combinations.

items.prompt: Examine the distribution of order values to understand purchasing behavior across customers. Create a histogram of order value and adjust bin sizes to reveal meaningful patterns. Annotate peaks to highlight the most common order ranges.

order_value.group_effects: Budget orders cluster around 10-40, Standard around 50-150, Premium around 200-400

df_spec.notes: Generate a multimodal distribution of order_value with clear peaks corresponding to customer segments and a few high-value outliers to make histogram binning and peak annotation meaningful.

items.prompt: Analyze how promotional periods impact sales trends over time by month. Plot sales as a line chart and visually highlight the date ranges where promotions were active. This should make it easy to see whether promotions correspond with noticeable increases in sales.

promotion_intensity.derived_from: 0 when is_promotion is false; higher values during promotion periods

df_spec.notes: Ensure promotions occur in contiguous multi-month blocks with elevated sales during those periods; non-promo months should have promotion_id='None' and intensity near zero.

items.prompt: Compare revenue and profit trends over time to assess overall business performance. Create side-by-side subplots showing monthly revenue in one chart and monthly profit in another. This layout should make it easier to spot differences in growth patterns between the two metrics.

revenue.group_effects: Enterprise higher and grows faster than Consumer

profit.group_effects: Consumer has slightly higher margin percentage

df_spec.notes: Each month should include both business units; profit should generally track revenue but allow occasional margin compression or expansion to highlight divergence.

items.prompt: Assess delivery time performance across regions or shipping methods to identify inconsistencies. Use box plots to show the distribution of delivery time for each group and annotate notable outliers. This will help highlight areas with unusually slow deliveries.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: Same-Day lowest, Express low, Standard highest; South and West slightly slower
delivery_time_days.noise: moderate with occasional extreme high values for outliers

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region and shipping method combination has sufficient samples. Inject ~5-10% extreme delivery_time_days values and flag them as is_outlier to support annotation of unusually slow deliveries.

items.prompt: Understand how key metrics like sales, profit, and ad spend relate to each other across the dataset. Create a correlation heatmap with labeled values to show the strength of relationships between these metrics. This should help identify which factors are most closely associated with performance.

df_spec.notes: Ensure meaningful correlations: ad_spend positively impacts sales, sales drives profit and customer_count, and higher discount_rate slightly boosts sales but reduces profit margin variability.

items.prompt: Show how monthly sales have trended across different regions over the past year using a multi-line view. I want to quickly identify which region is consistently outperforming others. Highlight the top-performing region so it stands out clearly for leadership review.

sales.group_effects: One region (e.g., North) consistently 20-30% higher than others

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has one record per month. Exactly one region should have is_top_region=true for all months and consistently higher sales to support visual highlighting.

items.prompt: Compare average profit across product categories to understand which areas of the business are most profitable. Include variability using error bars so we can assess consistency within each category. This should help identify categories with unstable or risky profit performance.

profit.group_effects: Electronics highest average profit, Furniture moderate, Clothing lower, Home & Kitchen moderate-high, Sports variable with wider spread
profit.noise: category-dependent variability (Sports and Furniture higher variance, Clothing more consistent)

df_spec.notes: Ensure each product category has sufficient observations (at least 40+) to compute meaningful averages and variability for error bars.

items.prompt: Analyze the relationship between customer spend and order frequency across different customer segments. Use distinct marker symbols for each segment so we can easily compare their behavior. The goal is to identify which segments drive higher revenue and how their purchasing patterns differ.

order_count.group_effects: VIP and High Value segments have higher order counts on average

total_spend.group_effects: VIP and High Value segments have significantly higher spend

avg_order_value.group_effects: VIP customers tend to have higher average order values

df_spec.notes: Ensure clear separation between segments in both spend and frequency while maintaining some overlap to reflect realistic customer behavior.

items.prompt: Visualize the monthly revenue trend over the past year to understand overall business performance. Mark and annotate the highest and lowest revenue points so we can investigate what drove those extremes. This will help highlight key periods of growth or decline.

revenue.group_effects: Enterprise and Online higher, SMB and Retail lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month has all regions. Compute total monthly revenue across regions to identify global max and min months, and mark those rows using is_max and is_min for annotation.

items.prompt: Show how website traffic from different channels has evolved over time on a monthly basis. Use a stacked area view to understand each channel’s contribution to total traffic. This should help us see which channels are driving growth and whether any are declining.

sessions.trend: Organic Search and Direct upward_over_time, Paid Search stable, Social slight upward, Email slight downward
sessions.group_effects: Organic Search highest overall, Email lowest

df_spec.notes: Each month should include all channels; values should allow clear stacked area differentiation and visible shifts in channel contribution over time.

items.prompt: Examine the distribution of order values across all transactions to understand customer spending behavior. Include a cumulative distribution line to show what proportion of orders falls below certain value thresholds. This will help us identify pricing opportunities and typical purchase ranges.

order_value.group_effects: VIP higher average order values, New lower

df_spec.notes: Order values should follow a right-skewed distribution to mimic real purchase behavior, with a few high-value outliers to make the cumulative distribution informative.

items.prompt: Compare how conversion rate and website traffic have changed over time on a monthly basis. Use a dual-axis view so we can see whether increases in traffic are actually leading to better conversion. The goal is to identify periods where traffic quality may have dropped or improved.

sessions.group_effects: Paid Search and Organic higher than Social and Referral

conversions.group_effects: Organic has slightly higher efficiency, Social lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure some periods where sessions increase but conversion_rate declines to simulate traffic quality drops; anomaly_flag should mark those months.

items.prompt: Build a dashboard-style view showing trends for revenue, profit, and number of orders over time by month. Each metric should be in its own subplot so we can compare patterns side by side. This will help us understand whether growth in orders is translating into higher profit and revenue.

df_spec.notes: Ensure each month has entries for all regions; profit should generally scale with revenue but include margin variability to test divergence between metrics.

items.prompt: Compare total sales across regions for each year to understand geographic performance trends. Use grouped bars so we can easily see how each region performs year over year. The goal is to identify regions with consistent growth or decline.

total_sales.trend: region-specific growth or decline over time
total_sales.group_effects: North and West higher baseline, South moderate, East and Central variable

df_spec.notes: Each region should have complete monthly data; yearly totals will be aggregated for grouped bar comparison across regions.

items.prompt: Analyze the relationship between revenue and cost across different products to identify efficient performers. Highlight the cluster of points where revenue is high but cost is low so we can spot top-performing products. This will help guide decisions on where to focus investment or expansion.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Home tend to have higher revenue

cost.group_effects: Apparel and Beauty tend to have relatively lower costs

df_spec.notes: Ensure a visible cluster where revenue is high (top 20%) and cost is low (bottom 30%), flagged via top_performer=true for highlighting in scatter plots.

items.prompt: Analyze how revenue has trended over time by month and assess the stability of those trends. Include a shaded confidence interval around the revenue line so we can understand uncertainty and volatility. Highlight any periods where variability is especially high to inform forecasting risk.

df_spec.notes: Ensure some contiguous periods exhibit elevated standard deviation to simulate high volatility; set high_variability_flag to true when revenue_std_dev exceeds a threshold (e.g., top 20%).

items.prompt: Examine the distribution of sales across different product categories to identify which categories have the most variability. Use a box plot to compare median sales, spread, and outliers by category. Make the layout clear enough to quickly spot categories with inconsistent performance.

sales_amount.group_effects: Electronics highest values with high variability, Clothing lower with tight spread, Home & Kitchen moderate spread, Sports moderate-high variability, Toys low with occasional spikes

df_spec.notes: Ensure each product category has sufficient observations and include occasional extreme values to make box plot outliers visible.

items.prompt: Identify the top-performing countries based on total sales and compare their contributions. Display a bar chart of the top countries with annotations showing exact sales values for quick reference. This should help stakeholders easily see which markets are driving the most revenue.

total_sales.group_effects: USA and Japan highest, followed by Germany and UK, others moderate

df_spec.notes: Ensure each country has data for every month so totals can be aggregated and ranked for bar chart visualization with annotations.

items.prompt: Evaluate how order volume varies by weekday and hour to uncover peak demand periods. Use a heatmap to show the density of orders across these time dimensions with a clear color scale. This will help operations teams optimize staffing and delivery planning.

order_count.group_effects: higher volumes during evening hours (17-21) and weekends

df_spec.notes: Ensure each weekday-hour combination appears multiple times across the date range to allow aggregation into a dense heatmap.

items.prompt: Compare revenue trends over time across multiple product categories to see which ones are growing or declining. Plot each category as a separate line so patterns and seasonality are easy to distinguish. The goal is to identify which categories are driving overall performance changes.

revenue.trend: varies_by_category (Electronics upward, Clothing slight decline, Home Goods stable, Sports seasonal upward, Toys strong seasonality with Q4 spikes)
revenue.group_effects: Electronics highest baseline, Toys and Clothing mid, Sports and Home Goods lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure each product category has a data point for every month; include clear seasonal patterns (e.g., Toys peaks in November-December) and differing trends across categories.

items.prompt: Investigate the relationship between revenue and profit across different regions to understand efficiency. Create a scatter plot with error bars to reflect variability or uncertainty in both metrics. This should highlight regions where high revenue does not translate into strong profitability.

revenue.group_effects: North America and Europe higher on average; Latin America and MEA lower

profit.group_effects: Asia Pacific and Latin America show weaker profit margins relative to revenue

df_spec.notes: Ensure some regions exhibit high revenue but disproportionately low or negative profit to highlight inefficiency; error columns represent variability for scatter error bars.

items.prompt: Break down total sales by product category and compare their percentage contributions across regions. Use a stacked bar chart to show how each category contributes to overall sales in each region. This will help identify dominant categories and gaps in the product mix.

sales.group_effects: Electronics and Food & Beverage higher overall; North America and Europe slightly higher totals

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has all product categories for every time period so percentage contributions can be computed reliably for stacked bar charts.

items.prompt: Track revenue trends over time by month and identify how major business events impacted performance. Add vertical lines to mark key events like promotions or product launches. This will help determine whether those events drove meaningful changes in revenue.

revenue.group_effects: months with event_flag=true show noticeable deviations

df_spec.notes: Ensure event_flag is true for a small subset of months with corresponding event_name and non-zero impact scores; revenue should reflect spikes or dips around those dates to support vertical event annotations.

items.prompt: Analyze the distribution of order values to understand customer purchasing behavior. Create a histogram of order amounts and overlay a normal distribution curve to assess how closely the data follows a typical pattern. This will help identify skewness and potential outliers in spending.

order_amount.group_effects: Premium and Business segments have higher average order values

df_spec.notes: Generate order_amount with a right-skewed distribution resembling log-normal behavior, including a small fraction of extreme high-value outliers flagged by is_outlier.

items.prompt: Compare the distribution of customer lifetime value across different customer segments. Use subplots of histograms so each segment can be evaluated side by side. This will help determine which segments drive the most value and how consistent their spending patterns are.

customer_lifetime_value.group_effects: Budget lowest, Standard moderate, Premium high, Enterprise highest with wider spread

df_spec.notes: Ensure each customer segment has sufficient representation and distinct distribution shapes, with Enterprise showing higher variance and occasional high-value outliers.

items.prompt: Track monthly revenue over time and compare it against cumulative revenue to understand long-term growth trends. Use a dual-axis view so we can see both period performance and overall accumulation clearly. Highlight any months where revenue growth significantly accelerates or slows down.

df_spec.notes: Ensure cumulative_revenue is strictly increasing. growth_acceleration_flag should be true when change in monthly_growth_rate exceeds a threshold (positive or negative) to highlight significant accelerations or slowdowns.

items.prompt: Compare total sales across product categories to identify which categories are driving the most revenue. Include detailed hover information such as profit, number of orders, and average order value for each category. The goal is to give stakeholders a richer understanding of category performance without cluttering the chart.

total_sales.group_effects: Electronics and Home & Kitchen higher on average

orders.group_effects: Clothing and Beauty tend to have higher order counts

df_spec.notes: Each category should have multiple rows to allow aggregation; total_sales should aggregate cleanly by category, and avg_order_value must remain consistent with total_sales and orders.

items.prompt: Analyze the relationship between advertising spend and revenue across different marketing channels. Highlight and annotate the top 5 data points with the highest revenue to understand what drives peak performance. This should help identify which channels deliver the best return at higher spend levels.

ad_spend.group_effects: Search and Social tend to have higher spend

revenue.group_effects: Search and Affiliate often yield higher revenue at similar spend

df_spec.notes: Ensure at least 5 records have the highest revenue values flagged as true in is_top_5_revenue to support annotation of peak performers.

items.prompt: Evaluate sales performance across product categories and months to identify seasonal trends and weak spots. Use a heatmap with annotated values so it’s easy to compare performance at a glance, and reverse the color scale to emphasize lower sales areas. The goal is to quickly spot underperforming periods that need attention.

sales.group_effects: Electronics and Grocery higher overall, Books and Toys lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure every product category has a value for each month to allow a complete heatmap grid with annotated sales values.

items.prompt: Build a multi-panel dashboard to monitor sales, profit, and conversion rate across regions over time. Each panel should focus on a different metric but align on the same time dimension for easy comparison. This should help leadership quickly assess overall performance and identify regions that are underperforming across multiple metrics.

df_spec.notes: Ensure each week has entries for all regions. Promo periods should temporarily boost visitors and conversion rate, creating visible spikes across aligned time-series panels.

items.prompt: Show how revenue trends evolve over time by month, using a filled area chart to emphasize growth patterns. Break this down by product category so we can see which categories contribute most to total revenue. The goal is to highlight both overall momentum and category-level contributions.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics highest, Beauty lowest, others moderate

df_spec.notes: Each month should include all product categories to enable stacked filled area visualization of revenue contribution over time.

items.prompt: Rank regions by total sales to identify the top-performing markets. Sort the bars in descending order and visually highlight the leading region to make it stand out. This should help quickly communicate which regions are driving the majority of revenue.

sales.group_effects: North and West have higher average sales, Central lowest

df_spec.notes: Data should be aggregated by region to compute total sales; is_top_region should be set true for the region with highest total sales after aggregation.

items.prompt: Compare the distribution of delivery time across different regions to understand variability in service performance. Use separate subplots for each region so differences in spread, median, and outliers are easy to spot. The goal is to identify regions with inconsistent or unusually slow delivery times.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: North and West have lower medians (~3-5 days), South moderate (~5-8 days), East higher variability with occasional long delays, Central highest variance with frequent outliers above 12 days

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has sufficient samples and distinct variance patterns, including occasional extreme delivery times to highlight outliers.

items.prompt: Examine the relationship between product price and number of orders across different categories. Use a scatter plot where color intensity represents total revenue to highlight high-value combinations. This will help identify which products balance strong demand with high revenue contribution.

price.group_effects: Electronics and Sports higher average price, Clothing and Beauty lower

orders.group_effects: Clothing and Beauty higher order volumes, Electronics lower

df_spec.notes: Ensure a mix of high-price low-order and low-price high-order products, with some high-price high-order outliers to highlight strong revenue performers.

items.prompt: Visualize how cumulative sales build over time by product category to understand contribution to overall growth. Use a stacked line chart to show how each category adds to total sales across months. The goal is to identify which categories consistently drive growth and which lag behind.

monthly_sales.group_effects: Electronics highest, Clothing and Home medium, Sports and Beauty lower

cumulative_sales.derived_from: cumulative sum of monthly_sales per product_category sorted by date

df_spec.notes: Ensure each category has a complete monthly sequence and cumulative_sales is strictly non-decreasing within each category.

items.prompt: Compare average delivery time across regions and product categories to understand where performance is inconsistent. Use grouped bars by category within each region and include error bars to show variability in delivery time. Add annotations to highlight the region-category combination with the worst average delivery time.

delivery_time_days.group_effects: West and Furniture have highest averages; East and Food have lowest; Electronics moderately high variability

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region-product_category combination has sufficient samples to compute mean and standard deviation for error bars; include at least one clearly worst-performing region-category pair.

items.prompt: Analyze how daily revenue has changed over time across different months and identify any major spikes or declines. Include interactive zooming so stakeholders can focus on specific date ranges like holiday periods or campaign windows. The goal is to quickly explore short-term vs long-term revenue trends.

revenue.group_effects: higher during holidays and campaigns, especially flash_sale

df_spec.notes: Ensure noticeable spikes during holidays and campaign periods, with occasional sharp declines after events. Keep baseline smooth and revenue volatile to highlight short-term vs long-term trends.

items.prompt: Examine the distribution of order values across different regions to see how customer spending behavior varies. Overlay multiple histograms for each region so we can compare spread, skewness, and typical order size. This should help identify regions with unusually high or low purchasing patterns.

order_value.group_effects: North higher average with moderate spread; South lower average with tighter spread; East right-skewed with occasional high outliers; West moderate average with wider variance

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has sufficient observations and distinct distribution shapes to make histogram overlays visually comparable.

items.prompt: Investigate the relationship between revenue and profit across different product categories. Include a scatter plot with marginal histograms to show the distribution of both metrics and highlight any correlation patterns. This will help identify categories that generate high revenue but low profit.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Automotive higher on average, Groceries lower

profit.group_effects: Furniture and Clothing higher margins, Electronics lower margins

units_sold.group_effects: Groceries high volume, Automotive low volume

df_spec.notes: Ensure some high-revenue but low-profit points (especially in Electronics) are flagged as outliers to highlight weak margin categories; maintain overall positive correlation with category-specific deviations.

items.prompt: Compare monthly orders and revenue trends to understand how sales volume translates into earnings over time. Use bars for orders and a line on a secondary axis for revenue, segmented by month. The goal is to identify periods where order growth does not align with revenue performance.

df_spec.notes: Revenue should generally track orders but include some months where avg_order_value shifts or promotions (promo_active) cause misalignment between order volume and revenue.

items.prompt: Evaluate sales performance across product categories and regions to identify clusters of similar behavior. Display a heatmap of revenue with hierarchical-style ordering to group similar regions and categories together. This should reveal patterns such as consistently strong or weak combinations.

revenue.group_effects: North, Northeast, and East higher for Electronics and Furniture; South, Southwest lower overall; Grocery stable across regions; Toys and Clothing moderate in Central and Midwest

df_spec.notes: Ensure full grid coverage for each region-product_category-date combination; revenue patterns should form clusters for hierarchical ordering.

items.prompt: Compare monthly revenue trends across multiple years to identify seasonality and year-over-year changes. Use subplots for each year so patterns can be easily compared side by side. The goal is to determine whether certain months consistently drive higher revenue.

revenue.group_effects: Q4 months (Oct-Dec) highest, Jan-Feb lowest, mild summer dip

df_spec.notes: Each year should contain all 12 months. Revenue should exhibit consistent seasonal peaks in Q4 and dips early in the year to make year-over-year comparisons visually meaningful.

items.prompt: Analyze total sales by region while also breaking down contributions by product category within each region. Use a combination of grouped and stacked bars to show both overall comparisons and internal composition. This will help identify which categories drive regional performance differences.

total_sales.group_effects: North and West higher overall; Electronics and Furniture contribute larger shares

df_spec.notes: Ensure each region has all product categories for every month so stacked bars sum cleanly and grouped comparisons across regions are consistent.

items.prompt: Explore the relationship between profit and revenue across products to identify outliers and unusual performance. Highlight and annotate products that have high revenue but low or negative profit. This analysis should help flag potential pricing or cost issues.

revenue.group_effects: Electronics and Home tend to have higher revenue

cost.group_effects: Some products intentionally have cost close to or exceeding revenue

df_spec.notes: Ensure a subset of products have high revenue but low or negative profit to act as clear outliers for annotation.

items.prompt: Assess how revenue and profit have evolved over time to understand margin trends. Use a dual-axis chart with both metrics plotted by month to compare their movement. The goal is to identify periods where profit diverges from revenue growth.

df_spec.notes: Ensure some periods where costs rise faster than revenue to create visible divergence between revenue and profit trends.
